title: ADK-204: Local Mocking & Agent Testing
description: Design regression suites, unit test tool integrations, and mock model endpoints to establish robust agent CI/CD pipelines.

highlights.title: Unit Testing Tools
highlights.description: Write local unit tests for tool functions to verify response accuracy without querying LLMs.

highlights.title: Model Mocking
highlights.description: Use mock responses to capture agent states and token payloads, avoiding live API invocation fees.

highlights.title: CI Pipeline Integration
highlights.description: Build regression testing scripts that integrate with GitHub Actions build verification steps.

modules.title: 1. Designing unit tests for custom tools

blocks.content: Agent tests are divided into tool function verification and orchestration flow verification. Unit testing your tool functions ensures bugs in your backend logic are caught independently of the LLM.

Question 1: Why test tool functions separately from the main agent routing logic?
- A) The LLM requires compiled Python binaries.
- B) It isolates programmatic logic bugs from stochastic LLM behavior.
- C) It avoids OAuth verification procedures.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Unit testing tools isolates system bugs from LLM randomness.

modules.title: 2. Mocking model responses and external API calls

blocks.content: Querying LLM APIs during unit tests increases expenses and introduces network dependencies. Mocking endpoints returns pre-configured strings, enabling offline tests.

blocks.code:
from unittest.mock import MagicMock
from google_adk import AgentRuntime

def test_agent_with_mock():
    runtime = AgentRuntime(config_path="agent.yaml")
    # Mock the internal execute method
    runtime.execute = MagicMock(return_value=MagicMock(content="Mocked response"))
    
    res = runtime.execute(prompt="Hello")
    assert res.content == "Mocked response"

blocks.content:
Question 1: What is the main benefit of mocking model responses in local test suites?
- A) It guarantees the model is 100% accurate.
- B) It prevents billing charges and network dependency errors during testing.
- C) It compiles Javascript dependencies.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Mocking makes tests cheap, deterministic, and fast.
Integration tests run the complete agent cycle (loading config, picking tools, calling models) using a sandbox server to assert overall task completion.

blocks.code:
def test_end_to_end_flow():
    runtime = AgentRuntime("agent.yaml")
    # The query requires a calculator tool
    res = runtime.execute("Solve: 125 * 4")
    assert "500" in res.content

blocks.title: Flaky Tests
blocks.content: LLMs can return varying text layouts. Use fuzzy assertions (like asserting substring existence) rather than absolute string matches.

Question 1: Why should you avoid strict string equality checks on agent text outputs?
- A) The SDK does not support strings.
- B) LLMs might rephrase responses slightly, leading to fragile, flaky tests.
- C) Responses are encrypted in memory.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Rephrasing makes exact matching unreliable; use substring checks or semantic evaluations.

modules.title: 4. Evaluating agent accuracy and regression metrics

blocks.content: As prompts and tools are modified, you must ensure the agent doesn't regress. Regression testing compares active runs against a benchmark list of known Q&A test cases.

items.title: Cosine Similarity
items.content: Measures semantic overlap of response embeddings against a golden standard.

items.title: Tool Call Auditing
items.content: Verifies the agent executes the correct tools in the correct order.

items.title: Assertion Passes
items.content: Runs boolean validation checks on returned data tables.

blocks.content:
Question 1: What is a 'golden dataset' in agent evaluation?
- A) An expensive dataset hosted on cloud nodes.
- B) A static collection of validated input-output pairs used to track regression.
- C) A system database storing user passwords.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Golden datasets benchmark model outputs.

modules.title: 5. Lab: Creating a continuous integration workflow

blocks.content: Lab: Creating a Continuous Integration Workflow
In this lab, you will configure a pytest configuration script that automatically tests custom math tools, executing them locally.

blocks.code:
import pytest
from google_adk.tools import tool

@tool
def dummy_add(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    """Adds two integers."""
    return a + b

def test_dummy_add():
    res = dummy_add(10, 20)
    assert res == 30
    
# Verify parameter schema registration
def test_tool_metadata():
    schema = dummy_add.schema
    assert schema["name"] == "dummy_add"
    assert "a" in schema["parameters"]

pip install pytest google-adk
pytest tests/test_agent.py

blocks.content:
Question 1: What does test_tool_metadata confirm?
- A) That the LLM returns the right calculations.
- B) That the function correctly parses to the expected JSON schema parameter structure.
- C) That the API key is active.

Feedback: Option B is correct. It tests the schema reflection engine.
